item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, it's an interesting concept and I think it's really well executed here, the last test was great

I only have a few small issues with it, that being that it kinda felt you shoehorned a GoI in (with the image caption) and it's tagged daevite it doesn't feel related to either in any way and if I were you I'd remove them or expand on it

Besides that it's really great so I'm gonna upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's a decent article. My only complaint is a pet peeve about the origins of the object being indeterminate when there clearly is some Arabic script engraved upon it (highly stylized and calligraphic, but writing all the same). It ought to at least be mentioned in the description.

Also, why is the article tagged "daevite" when there's neither mention of nor allusion to the Daevites anywhere in the article?

Novote for now, but I would upvote if the issues I mentioned would be addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this one wasn't the best written, but the concept is pretty good. No vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally still feel you could've chose something more poignant. perhaps something related to time. not something as cliched as an hourglass… I've heard of something like an incense clock, which would pull double-duty on the meaningfulness side.

though that point isn't enough to effect my vote.

What really bothers me is that I've seen this concept before on the site, a few times. I don't take issue with the concept but I feel it needs some sort of twist to it to keep it interesting. The articles I've linked all have their own spins on this idea and that's why they work fairly well.

As just a random idea; why not have an innately malevolent object that doesn't predict the time of death but instead SETS it. It doesn't need to be as sweeping a change as that but that's the sort of thing I would be looking for.

but for now, -1. sorry :|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The reliance on scheduled terminations is a bit iffy, but the comment about amnestic recycling makes it clear that killing 'em is unusual. It's also necessary for the payoff in the last experiment, and that payoff is *really good*.

There are a few bits of slight confusion though. "Both reviews" in the second log makes no sense, for instance. But those are minor cleanup, not fundamental to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate that you told me why you downvoted - it's very helpful.

So, elephant in the room: there are a bunch of different canons regarding how often D-Class are terminated, ranging from "monthly" to "only when it's specifically called for, people are hard to acquire, are you insane". Is your dislike of "routine termination" based on this sentiment?

My personal canon is far on the "only when it's specifically called for" side, but I also like to think that the Foundation pretends that it terminates D-Class more than it really does. It's a useful psychological tool, to convince the D-Class that they need perfect behavior to avoid their dirt-nap: otherwise they'd need to work a lot harder to keep people in line.

On the other hand, there are occasionally going to be D-Class that have outlived their usefullness, either due to consistently uncontrollable behavior across multiple amnestic cycles, terminal diseases which will be uneconomical to treat and render the individual useless for tests, or expensive injuries incurred during testing. When a test requires a D-Class for whom the death date can be confidently known, either because the test will cause fatal symptoms or has predictive or retrocausal effects which the test is designed to establish the limits of, this smaller pool of schedulable D-Class can be used.

Back to this SCP, I hinted at the above backstory when I talked about needing D-Class whose terminations have been scheduled: this was intended to suggest, because the instructions were necessary in the first place, that this was an unusual or difficult requirement. I think I did a bad job of communicating this (and I think in my zeal to have a spooky "ooo, the skip was more accurate than the Foundation's plan" I unintentionally went too far), so I've made an edit which will hopefully make both of us happier.

And, heck, maybe you'll still hate it. Fair's fair. In any case, thanks for the feedback. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The heavy reliance on "routine termination" kills this for me.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, because that's not what this skip is.

It's not supposed to be a scary object. It's not supposed to strike terror into your heart when you see it. It's supposed to be a nondescript object that you could encounter anywhere, and you might pick it up and handle it, or see if there's any ink inside. And before you know it, boom, you're helplessly rattling off the minutes you have left, for as many minutes as you have left.

The horror comes from the terrible consequences of something seemingly so mundane. And to be clear, this thing does not kill you: it just tells you how much longer you'll get.

item.title: (no title)
item.wikidot:authorName: S i l e n c e
item.content:encoded:
The anomaly seems to be not directly related to the ink bottle.
So why not use more powerful objects? e.g. an ugly and ridiculous ceramic doll, Metaphor is the same as the incarnation of death.

item.title: Photo Credit
item.content:encoded:
I pulled the image from the Wikimedia Foundation here:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Inkwell,_Iran,_Khorasan,_late_12th_century_AD,_bronze,_silver,_and_copper-inlaid_-_Aga_Khan_Museum_-_Toronto,_Canada_-_DSC06350.jpg
